text: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use (5th edition, 2015)
Overview: Categorization System

The Medical Eligibility Criteria (MEC) categorize contraceptive methods based on the safety of use for women with specific characteristics and medical conditions. Categories range from 1 (use without restriction) to 4 (use is contraindicated due to serious risk).

caption: WHO MEC Categories and Definitions
rows: A condition for which there is no restriction for the use of the contraceptive method
Advantages of using the method generally outweigh theoretical or proven risks
Use the method but provide counselling
Theoretical or proven risks usually outweigh the advantages of using the method
Use of method not usually recommended unless other methods unavailable
Unacceptable health risk if the contraceptive method is used; the contraceptive method is contraindicated

text: Combined Oral Contraceptives (COCs)

Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) contain both estrogen and progestin. They work primarily through suppression of ovulation. COCs must be taken daily and have 7-day hormone-free intervals in most regimens.
COC Eligibility: Key Conditions

caption: WHO MEC for Combined Oral Contraceptives
rows: Use with caution; monitor cardiovascular risk factors
Significantly increased stroke risk; use progestin-only methods instead
Can use COCs; counsel on warning signs
Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) with antiphospholipid antibodies
Absolute contraindication due to VTE risk
Current deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism (PE)
Contraindicated; use barrier methods or IUD
Depends on duration; consider temporary alternatives

text: Progestin-Only Pills (POPs)

Progestin-only pills (POPs), also called the 'mini-pill', contain only progestin with no estrogen. They work by thickening cervical mucus, thinning the endometrium, and suppressing ovulation in some users. POPs must be taken daily at the same time within a 3-hour window.
POP Eligibility

POPs are safer for women with contraindications to estrogen-containing methods. Most medical conditions that are Category 3-4 for COCs are Category 1-2 for POPs, making them excellent alternatives for breastfeeding women, those over 35 who smoke, and those with migraine with aura.

caption: WHO MEC for Progestin-Only Pills
rows: Safe and effective; no reduced milk production
Unlike COCs, no stroke risk increase
POP does not increase VTE risk

text: Depot Medroxyprogesterone Acetate (DMPA)

DMPA (Depo-Provera) is a long-acting injectable contraceptive given as an intramuscular or subcutaneous injection every 12 weeks (3 months). It works primarily by suppressing ovulation. DMPA is highly effective (>99%) and does not require daily user action.

caption: WHO MEC for DMPA Injection
rows: Age 18-45 years without other risk factors
Safe and effective for most women
Safe; no impact on milk production
Personal history of migraine with aura
Caution advised; monitor BP; preferred over COCs
Safe; does not increase VTE risk unlike estrogens
Wait 48 hours to rule out pregnancy

text: Intrauterine Devices (IUDs)

Intrauterine devices (IUDs) are long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs) inserted into the uterine cavity. Two types exist: copper IUDs (effective 10+ years, work through copper toxicity to sperm) and levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs (LNG-IUD, effective 3-7 years, work through hormonal + local effects).
IUD Eligibility and Safety

IUDs are among the safest contraceptives with minimal medical contraindications. They can be used by most women regardless of age, parity, or medical conditions. Key contraindications relate to current or recent reproductive tract infection.

caption: WHO MEC for IUDs (Copper and LNG)
rows: Wait 3 months; category 2 at 3+ months postpartum
Can insert; optimal timing is 4+ weeks postpartum
Can insert but higher infection risk; monitor closely
IUD safe; no increased ectopic risk
IUDs safe across entire reproductive lifespan
Defer insertion until treated; screen before insertion

text: Barrier Methods (Condoms, Diaphragm)

Barrier methods include male and female condoms, diaphragms, and cervical caps. These methods work by physically blocking sperm from reaching the egg. Condoms have the added benefit of STI prevention. No medical contraindications exist for barrier method use.

caption: WHO MEC for Barrier Methods
rows: No medical contraindications; safe for all women
Can begin anytime; no pregnancy risk in postpartum period

text: Sterilization (Female and Male)

Permanent contraceptive methods include tubal ligation (female sterilization) and vasectomy (male sterilization). These are highly effective (>99%) and permanent. Decision for sterilization should be made with careful counselling and informed consent.

WHO MEC does not provide categorical recommendations for sterilization as it is a permanent, non-reversible procedure. Eligibility depends on informed choice, counselling, and absence of acute illness or active infection. No absolute medical contraindications exist once acute conditions resolve.
Emergency Contraception

Emergency contraception (EC) refers to methods used to prevent pregnancy after unprotected intercourse. Methods include levonorgestrel pills (Plan B), ulipristal acetate (ella), and copper IUD insertion. EC is most effective when initiated within 72 hours of intercourse (IUD effective up to 5 days).

caption: WHO MEC for Emergency Contraception
rows: Available without prescription; most accessible option
More effective than levonorgestrel; requires prescription in some countries
Most effective EC; also provides ongoing contraception

text: Counselling and Method Selection

Choice of contraceptive method should be based on: (1) woman's reproductive goals and desired family size, (2) her medical eligibility (using WHO MEC), (3) her preferences and cultural considerations, (4) accessibility and cost, and (5) side effect profile tolerance.

Effective counselling includes discussing all available methods, their efficacy, side effects, duration of action, reversibility, and STI protection (only condoms). Women should be counselled that no contraceptive method is 100% effective and that backup methods or emergency contraception may be needed.
